learning_outcomes.description: Successfully use a range of music creation apps and software.
Collaborate with others within contexts which involve music technology.
Record, mix and produce audio and MIDI using professional audio software and equipment.
reflect critically on practice, analyse and evaluate experiences and actions.
Understand the ways music technology is employed across professional and classroom contexts.

assessments.due_date: Workshops will be held in timetabled Lab classes during Weeks 4, 5, and 6. Students will form pairs and self-allocate a workshop date and topic during Week 2 via an online sign-up sheet. From Week 3 onward, the allocated date is firm and can only be changed via an official extension request.
assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Practical/ Demonstration, Presentation
assessments.task_description:
Facilitate a Workshop

In a group of two, facilitate a 30-minute workshop during your scheduled lab class, introducing peers to a particular technology, app, or software.


Timeline

In Week 2: Self-allocate a workshop date and topic via the sign-up sheet.

Before your allocated workshop date: Conduct independent research to thoroughly learn and understand the allocated technology. Plan an engaging 30-minute workshop session, including demonstrations, explanations, and interactivity.

On your allocated workshop date: With your partner, facilitate a 30-minute workshop on your allocated topic. Take charge of timing, classroom engagement, and technology management. Submit your workshop plan to Blackboard.

During Weeks 4, 5, and 6: Participate in classmates' workshops.


Topics

A range of workshop topics will be be available for allocation, including:

* iPad Music Apps (e.g. Koala Sampler, ThumbJam, Loopy HD)

* Music Hardware (e.g. Ableton Push, Korg Volca, Zoom H4n)

* Music Software (e.g. Audacity, MuseScore, Live, Max for Live)

* Free choice (consult with Tutor)

Where topics are multi-faceted and complex (e.g. Ableton Live or Ableton Push), students should choose to focus on one aspect (e.g. multi-track recording in Ableton Live; Playing Drums on Ableton Push).


Presentation and Submission

Presentations must occur in-person on the allocated date with all group members present for the entire duration of the class. A workshop plan must be submitted on the day which details the main sections, talking points, and activities to be covered in the workshop; this may take the form of a document or slides. Students must attend and participate in at least one class of workshops other than the class in which they are scheduled to present in order to pass the "Participation" criterion.


Marking Criteria

* Operation 30%

* Technical Understanding 30%

* Communication 20%

* Participation (in others' workshops) 20%


This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit your workshop plan (maximum 5 pages) to blackboard on your workshop date.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Each student will propose and complete a creative project using music technology. This will involve a creative output as well as a written exegesis.

The assessment of this project is broken into four parts: a Project Proposal, Project Journals, Project Presentation, and Project Exegesis.

Project Proposal

Students submit a Project Proposal in Week 8, setting out the project aims, technology to be used, and the skills required, using the template provided.

This is a 10% graded assessment item.

Project Journal 1 & 2

From Weeks 9 to 11, students work on projects. With tutor’s guidance, the focus is on gaining the required skills and completing the creative work.

During this time, students keep a Project Journal to help reflect on the project, submitting journal entries in Weeks 10 and 12.

Each of these are a 5% pass/fail assessment item.

Project Presentation

Students present the creative output of your project to the class in the Week 13 Project Presentation. This must be done in person during the Week 13 class time*.

This is a 30% graded assessment item.

Project Exegesis

Finally, students write and submit a 2000-word Project Exegesis. This is a scholarly work reflecting on the project and linking it to existing research.

This is a 20% graded assessment item.


*In the case of projects which do not suit in-class presentation, (e.g. a concert or sound installation), alternative arrangements must be made and agreed upon with the tutor and course co-ordinator.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.
